Title: Glycinebetaine protects plants against abiotic stress: mechanisms and biotechnological applications

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely transports GB with high affinity when expressed in yeast
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Tomato LeProT1 transported GB with high affinity in yeast. 2. It also transported proline and GABA with lower affinity. 3. This suggests some proline transporters may mediate GB transport in plants.
Common_Genes.Applied_Species: Yeast assay system; tomato as source
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast transport assays
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Compatible solute transport contributing to stress protection
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for improving GB transport and compartmentation in engineered plants
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No plant GB-specific transporter has been reported according to the review.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely mediates GB transport; GB strongly inhibits its GABA transport
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis ProT2 is a GABA transporter whose activity is strongly inhibited by GB. 2. This indicates strong affinity of GB for the transporter. 3. ProT2-like transporters may contribute to GB transport in plant cells.
Common_Genes.Applied_Species: Yeast assay system; Arabidopsis as source
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for improving GB transport/partitioning
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evidence is indirect via inhibition and affinity rather than definitive in planta GB transport demonstration.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Does not appear to transport GB
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HvProT was cloned from roots of salt-stressed barley. 2. Uptake of proline by yeast expressing HvProT was not inhibited by GB. 3. This suggests HvProT does not act as a GB transporter.
Common_Genes.Applied_Species: Yeast assay system; barley as source
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful negative example when selecting transporters for GB engineering
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review explicitly evaluates its non-role in GB transport.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Barley BADH isoform contributing to GB synthesis, possibly in peroxisomes under salt stress
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Barley contains two BADH genes, BBD1 and BBD2. 2. BBD1 encodes a peroxisomal enzyme. 3. BBD1 transcripts accumulate in root epidermal cells under salt stress, supporting non-chloroplastic GB biosynthesis in barley.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; subcellular localization; transcript localization under salt stress
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Root epidermal cells under salt stress
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Root epidermal cells under salt stress
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Vascular tissues and roots inferred GB synthesis sites
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports compartment-specific engineering of GB synthesis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Challenges the assumption that GB synthesis occurs exclusively in chloroplasts.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Barley BADH isoform associated with cytosolic/vascular GB biosynthesis under salt stress
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BBD2 is the second barley BADH gene. 2. It encodes a cytosolic enzyme and its transcripts rise considerably in vascular parenchyma of leaves and pericycle of roots under salt stress. 3. Findings indicate GB synthesis in vascular tissues and support cytosolic synthesis in barley.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; subcellular localization; cell-specific transcript analysis under salt stress
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaf vascular parenchyma and root pericycle under salt stress
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Leaf vascular parenchyma and root pericycle under salt stress
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Vascular tissues and roots inferred GB synthesis sites
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant for engineering tissue-specific GB biosynthesis and transport
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review highlights BBD2 as especially induced by salt stress in young leaves and roots.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for GB-induced chilling tolerance and ROS control in Arabidopsis roots
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FRO2 is a GB-up-regulated gene in Arabidopsis roots. 2. FRO2-null mutant failed to respond to GB in chilling recovery and ROS suppression assays. 3. GB had major post-transcriptional effects on FRO2 activity in the cold.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: GB treatment; mutant analysis; chilling assays; ferric reductase activity measurement
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression alone did not enhance chilling tolerance
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential component to stack with GB engineering for improved chilling tolerance
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts in GB response rather than GB synthesis.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: GB-up-regulated ROS-scavenging enzyme associated with reduced H2O2 accumulation and chilling tolerance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CAT2 was identified as a GB-up-regulated gene in Arabidopsis roots. 2. Review links GB treatment or GB accumulation with increased catalase activity in several systems. 3. Catalase induction is proposed as a major mechanism by which GB lowers ROS and enhances stress tolerance.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: GB-responsive expression analysis; enzyme activity assays in cited studies
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Mitochondrion per review text for catalase 2 entry
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate downstream marker/effector of GB-mediated antioxidant defense
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray/root GB-up-regulated gene context; accession NA
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review also mentions CAT3 in maize and cat1 in tomato as GB/ROS-responsive catalases in related contexts.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point in GB-deficient plants; absence of CMO is described as a primary constraint on GB synthesis in tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes glycinebetaine accumulation and enhances abiotic stress tolerance when introduced into non-accumulators, but accumulation is often limited by choline supply
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CMO catalyzes the first oxidation step from choline to betaine aldehyde in plant GB biosynthesis. 2. In tobacco, lack of CMO is identified as a primary constraint on GB synthesis. 3. Constitutive expression of spinach CMO in tobacco produced only low GB unless choline/phosphocholine was supplied, indicating substrate limitation.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Spinach; sugar beet; various GB-accumulating plants
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Nicotiana tabacum; Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression; metabolite profiling with and without choline feeding; stress tolerance assays
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast in many species; uncertain/undetected chloroplast activity in some species such as barley and mangrove
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Constitutive promoters in reported transgenics; plastid expression in some studies
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Salt stress; drought; temperature stress; choline or phosphocholine feeding
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Low GB accumulation due to endogenous choline limitation and possible chloroplast choline import limitation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering GB biosynthesis, especially when combined with choline-supply engineering such as PEAMT overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, especially chloroplasts; high accumulation also reported in reproductive organs in GB-engineered plants
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Abiotic stress protection including salt, drought, chilling, freezing and heat tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes that some natural GB accumulators show no detectable chloroplast CMO activity, implying alternative enzymes may catalyze the first oxidation step.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes betaine aldehyde to glycinebetaine using NAD+
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important terminal enzyme; not established as sole rate-limiting step across species
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes GB accumulation and enhances tolerance to salt, heat, cold and other abiotic stresses in transgenic plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BADH catalyzes the second oxidation step to produce GB. 2. Single-gene BADH transformation was sufficient in several non-GB accumulators to confer GB accumulation, implying endogenous first-step activity may exist. 3. BADH transgenics showed improved photosynthesis and stress tolerance, including better Rubisco/Rubisco activase function and stronger antioxidant defense under heat and salt stress.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Spinacia oleracea; Atriplex nummularia; Hordeum vulgare
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Tobacco; Arabidopsis; Rice; Carrot; Tomato; Wheat
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Nicotiana tabacum; Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Daucus carota; Solanum lycopersicum; Triticum aestivum
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; metabolite profiling; chlorophyll fluorescence; CO2 assimilation assays; enzyme activity assays under stress
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast in some species/transgenics; peroxisome for barley BBD1; cytosol for barley BBD2
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In barley under salt stress, BBD2 transcripts rise in vascular parenchyma of leaves and pericycle of roots; BBD1 in root epidermis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Salt-induced in barley vascular tissues and roots
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Various constitutive and plastid-expression systems in transgenics
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Salt stress; heat stress; cold stress; exogenous betaine aldehyde in some rice studies
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong engineering value for direct GB production, especially with proper subcellular targeting and precursor provision
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CMO; Rubisco activase (physiological association via GB effects, not direct binding)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves/chloroplasts; reproductive organs in GB-engineered plants
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Abiotic stress protection and maintenance of photosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BADH may also oxidize/detoxify dimethylsulfoniopropionaldehyde and aminoaldehydes; review highlights multifunctionality.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Choline supply pathway feeding glycinebetaine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the three methylation steps converting phosphoethanolamine to phosphocholine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for choline and thus GB synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Presented as the key enzyme of choline synthesis in plants and likely a major limitation in non-GB accumulators such as tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases phosphocholine, choline and downstream GB accumulation when overexpressed in engineered tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PEAMT is described as the key enzyme of plant choline synthesis. 2. Overexpression of spinach PEAMT in tobacco increased phosphocholine 5-fold and choline 50-fold. 3. This enhancement resulted in about 30-fold higher GB accumulation via the engineered pathway, indicating precursor supply strongly limits GB engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; metabolite profiling of phosphocholine, choline and GB
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Comparison of endogenous versus engineered choline supply; phosphocholine feedback context
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Elevated endogenous phosphocholine can strongly inhibit PEAMT in vitro, potentially constraining GB synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High value as a stacking partner with CMO/BADH/codA/cox to overcome precursor limitation in GB engineering
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports GB accumulation for abiotic stress tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper discusses maize and sorghum GB deficiency in relation to phosphocholine-mediated inhibition of PEAMT.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Confers GB accumulation and improved tolerance to drought, chilling and salt stress in multiple transgenic crops
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. betA encodes bacterial choline dehydrogenase for the first oxidation step toward GB. 2. Expression in maize, tobacco and rice increased GB accumulation and abiotic stress tolerance. 3. In maize, cytosolic betA improved chilling and drought tolerance and protected reproductive performance under drought.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Zea mays; Nicotiana tabacum; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Maize inbred DH4866; tobacco cv. Xanthi mentioned elsewhere in review for stress physiology, not necessarily all betA studies
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; GB quantification; photosynthesis assays; field drought evaluation
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol; modified construct targeted to mitochondria in rice
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No major undesirable phenotype under non-stress conditions reported for GB-accumulating lines
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for crop engineering of stress tolerance, including reproductive-stage drought protection in maize
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves; reproductive organs may also benefit via translocation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In maize, elevated total leaf GB did not necessarily raise chloroplast GB, leaving mechanism of cytosolic protection unresolved.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Converts precursor to end product via betaine aldehyde intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes choline to betaine aldehyde and then further to glycinebetaine
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; emphasized as the most extensively tested engineering gene
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases GB accumulation and broadly enhances tolerance to salt, chilling, freezing, heat, oxidative and light stress, often with strong effects when targeted to chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. codA from Arthrobacter enables single-gene GB engineering in plants. 2. codA-transgenic plants were the most extensively studied and showed enhanced abiotic stress tolerance across the life cycle. 3. Chloroplast targeting often gave stronger protection than cytosolic targeting, even with lower total GB. 4. codA lines also showed beneficial reproductive traits such as more seeds in Arabidopsis and larger flowers/fruits in tomato under non-stress conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; Rice; Tomato; Potato; Brassica juncea; Persimmon; Synechococcus
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Solanum lycopersicum; Solanum tuberosum; Brassica juncea; Diospyros kaki; Synechococcus sp. PCC 7942
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; subcellular targeting comparisons; GB quantification; chlorophyll fluorescence; stress survival; reproductive trait analysis; microarray in some codA rice/tomato studies
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast, cytosol, or both depending on construct; cytosol in cyanobacteria
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S constitutive promoter in Arabidopsis and tomato; various promoters in other studies
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Salt, chilling, freezing, strong light, oxidative stress, heat, drought
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Generally no undesirable growth phenotypes under non-stress conditions reported; in tomato fewer flowers per inflorescence and lower fruit-set without hand pollination despite larger flowers/fruits
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Top candidate for GB bioengineering; chloroplast targeting is especially effective; useful for stress tolerance and potentially yield/reproductive improvement
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Strongly associated with chloroplast accumulation; high levels also in flowers, siliques, shoot apices and fruit locular cavities in some transgenics
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Abiotic stress protection and support of reproductive success
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray analysis reported for codA-transgenic tomato flower buds and codA-transgenic rice under water stress; accession numbers not provided in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Central summarized engineering gene of the review; despite low GB levels, substantial stress tolerance suggests regulatory effects beyond osmotic adjustment.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes choline to glycinebetaine via betaine aldehyde
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances GB accumulation and stress tolerance in Arabidopsis, Brassica, tobacco and rice
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cox from Arthrobacter pascens was used to engineer GB synthesis in several plants. 2. In rice, both stress-inducible and constitutive promoters drove chloroplast-targeted cox, with constitutive lines accumulating slightly more GB but stress-inducible lines showing stronger stress tolerance than expected from GB amount alone. 3. Results support both metabolic and signaling roles of GB.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Brassica napus; Nicotiana tabacum; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; promoter comparison; GB measurement; salinity tolerance assays
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol or chloroplast depending on construct
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Stress-inducible promoter (SIP); ubiquitin promoter (UBI, constitutive) in rice
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Salt stress; drought; freezing; saline growth conditions
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful engineering gene; promoter choice affects stress performance beyond absolute GB content
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review distinguishes cox from codA as bacterial choline oxidase genes used in different studies.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates glycine to sarcosine and also catalyzes methylation of sarcosine in the intermediate step
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies intermediate pool toward target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: In combination with SDMT enables GB production and enhances tolerance to salt, chilling and CuSO4 stress in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GSMT is part of the bacterial glycine-methylation route to GB. 2. Co-expression with SDMT from Aphanothece halophytica produced GB in Arabidopsis. 3. Maximum GB accumulation remained around 2.0 μmol g−1 FW with NaCl and glycine feeding, not obviously superior to choline-oxidation routes.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic co-expression with SDMT; GB quantification; stress tolerance assays
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requires glycine supply; utility may not exceed choline-oxidizing enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Alternative route for GB engineering, but less favored than codA/cox/CMO-BADH routes based on summarized evidence
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review also refers to transgene as ApGSMT.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates sarcosine to dimethylglycine and dimethylglycine to glycinebetaine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product in final methylation step
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: With GSMT supports GB accumulation and improved abiotic stress tolerance in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SDMT catalyzes the final methylation step to GB in the glycine route. 2. Co-expression with GSMT in Arabidopsis produced GB and improved tolerance to salt, chilling and CuSO4. 3. Overall engineering utility did not clearly surpass choline-oxidation enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic co-expression with GSMT; GB quantification; stress tolerance assays
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requires precursor supply and co-expression partner
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Alternative GB-engineering module but likely less practical than codA or precursor-enhanced choline oxidation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review text also refers to ApSDMT/ApDMT nomenclature.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Stress/GB-responsive regulation associated with membrane trafficking and chilling recovery
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ROS-associated stress response linked to GB-induced chilling tolerance
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for GB-induced chilling tolerance in Arabidopsis roots
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Not a biosynthetic regulator of GB itself, but required for the biological effect of GB on chilling tolerance
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RabA4c was identified as a GB-up-regulated gene in Arabidopsis roots. 2. GB enhanced expression of RabA4c 24 h after treatment. 3. In RabA4c-disrupted mutants, GB failed to enhance recovery growth after chilling, indicating RabA4c is required for GB-induced tolerance.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: GB treatment; Northern blotting; mutant analysis under chilling recovery assays
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Roots, induced 24 h after GB treatment
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential marker/mechanistic component for GB-responsive stress tolerance pathways
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray analysis in Arabidopsis roots; accession NA
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts downstream of GB signaling rather than controlling GB biosynthesis.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional regulation in GB-induced chilling tolerance pathway
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: GB-responsive stress gene expression associated with ROS control
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for GB-induced chilling tolerance according to cited Arabidopsis studies
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Part of the GB-responsive regulatory module required for chilling tolerance, not a direct controller of GB biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A bZIP transcription factor was identified among proteins required for GB-induced chilling tolerance in Arabidopsis. 2. It is part of the small set of genes strongly associated with GB-mediated suppression of ROS and chilling injury. 3. Evidence comes from GB-responsive gene expression analyses summarized in the review.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: GB-responsive expression analysis summarized from mutant/expression studies
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulator for strengthening GB-responsive stress defense pathways
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray/root GB-up-regulated gene context; accession NA
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review gives limited mechanistic detail beyond its requirement in GB-induced chilling tolerance.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Stress tolerance and antioxidant defense, including catalase-associated responses
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: CATALASE1 (cat1) and other stress genes implied
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Associated with elevated catalase activity and cat1 expression in tomato
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Background comparator gene showing stress tolerance with growth penalties; not central to GB metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Not decisive for GB production; included because review explicitly compares GB engineering with CBF1-mediated stress tolerance and associated CAT induction
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of Arabidopsis CBF1 in tomato enhanced tolerance to chilling, oxidative and water stress. 2. This tolerance was associated with increased catalase activity and cat1 expression. 3. Unlike GB engineering, CBF1 overexpression caused dwarfism and reduced reproductive performance under non-stress conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; stress tolerance assays; catalase activity and transcript analysis
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: GB used as comparison for stress protection
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Dwarfism; decreased fruit-set; fewer seeds per fruit
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Serves as contrast showing GB engineering may avoid common growth penalties of stress TF engineering
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a core GB biosynthetic gene; included cautiously as a regulator highlighted in the review's engineering comparison.
